In Pakistan, two major Late Mesozoic Neo-Tethyan suture zones, the Western Suture Zone (WSZ) and Indus Suture Zone (ISZ), delineate the remnants of the Neo-Tethyan Ocean. These suture zones and associated ophiolites document the rift-drift history, northward migration of the Indian Plate, and geodynamic evolution of the India-Eurasia collision. This review aims to refine the geodynamic model of Pakistani ophiolites and contribute to the reconstruction of Neo-Tethyan tectonic evolution and the Alpine-Himalayan orogenic system. The WSZ ophiolites, located along the west-northwest boundary of the Indian Plate, contains well-preserved Penrose-style sequences, such as those near Bela, Muslim Bagh, and Waziristan. These ophiolites are interpreted to be formed first at ridge-like spreading centers and later in supra-subduction zones (SSZ) during the northwestward subduction of the Neo-Tethys oceanic slab beneath the southern margin of the Eurasian Plate during subduction initiation Cretaceous. The ISZ comprises highly fragmented ophiolitic mélanges, including the Dargai and Shangla-Mingora complexes, which reflect complex intra-oceanic subduction, accretion, and collision processes along the Kohistan Island Arc. These sutures record the tectonic evolution of the Neo-Tethyan Ocean, with the complied petrological, geochemical, and geochronological data indicating a transition initially from mid-ocean ridge basalt (MORB) to island-arc tholeiite (IAT). The geochemical diversity in their mantle sections, ranging from MORB to IAT and boninitic signatures, reflects varied magmatic processes during subduction initiation. The emplacement of these ophiolites, influenced by oblique convergence and transpression in the WSZ and by collision-driven accretion in the ISZ, illustrates the complex tectonics associated with closure of the Neo-Tethyan Ocean.

item.title: Extensive fractional crystallization in strongly peraluminous granitoids recorded by zircon Zr isotopes
item.dc:creator: Ze Liu
Nicole Xike Nie
Oliver Jagoutz
Santiago Tassara
Jay J. Ague
Jin-Cheng Xie
Liang-Liang Zhang
Qing Wang
Di-Cheng Zhu
item.description: Strongly peraluminous granitoids, classically considered “S-type” melts derived from metasedimentary sources, often exhibit large compositional variations, particularly in their alumina saturation and maficity. Such variations are generally attributed to (1) selective entrainment and/or unmixing of restitic and/or peritectic minerals in anatectic melts or (2) fractional crystallization (FC) of a parental magma. Distinguishing between these two models solely based on chemical compositions is challenging. Here, we use zircon Zr stable isotopes ( δ 94/90 Zr relative to NIST RM 8299) to investigate the petrogenesis of the archetypal Kinsman granitoids and associated ultramafic–mafic cumulates from the New England Appalachians, USA. Autocrystic zircon from the Kinsman granitoids records a ∼ 0.3‰ increase in δ 94/90 Zr NIST values (from –0.23 to +0.12‰) that correlates with increasing whole-rock SiO 2 (58–76 wt%). This systematic isotopic evolution, which is decoupled from zircon ε Hf (t), negatively correlates with magma temperature (688–812°C). These characteristics argue against open-system processes such as restite entrainment or continuous crustal assimilation during magma ascent in the crust. Instead, they suggest a nearly closed-system evolution dominated by FC. The lower δ 94/90 Zr NIST values (–0.45 to –0.04‰) of zircon from associated olivine-pyroxene hornblendites and orthopyroxenites are consistent with their origin as earlier cumulates genetically linked to the Kinsman granitoids. Our results demonstrate that while sediment assimilation in the lower crust could be responsible for the peraluminous signature, FC is the dominant driver of compositional diversity in these systems. Published: 2026-03-21T23:29:37+00:00 Venue: Geochimica et Cosmochimica Acta Score: 0.565 (consider)
item.content:encoded:
Authors: Ze Liu; Nicole Xike Nie; Oliver Jagoutz; Santiago Tassara; Jay J. Ague; Jin-Cheng Xie; Liang-Liang Zhang; Qing Wang; Di-Cheng Zhu

Journal: Geochimica et Cosmochimica Acta

DOI: 10.1016/j.gca.2026.03.034

Relevance: 0.565 (consider)

Strongly peraluminous granitoids, classically considered “S-type” melts derived from metasedimentary sources, often exhibit large compositional variations, particularly in their alumina saturation and maficity. Such variations are generally attributed to (1) selective entrainment and/or unmixing of restitic and/or peritectic minerals in anatectic melts or (2) fractional crystallization (FC) of a parental magma. Distinguishing between these two models solely based on chemical compositions is challenging. Here, we use zircon Zr stable isotopes ( δ 94/90 Zr relative to NIST RM 8299) to investigate the petrogenesis of the archetypal Kinsman granitoids and associated ultramafic–mafic cumulates from the New England Appalachians, USA. Autocrystic zircon from the Kinsman granitoids records a ∼ 0.3‰ increase in δ 94/90 Zr NIST values (from –0.23 to +0.12‰) that correlates with increasing whole-rock SiO 2 (58–76 wt%). This systematic isotopic evolution, which is decoupled from zircon ε Hf (t), negatively correlates with magma temperature (688–812°C). These characteristics argue against open-system processes such as restite entrainment or continuous crustal assimilation during magma ascent in the crust. Instead, they suggest a nearly closed-system evolution dominated by FC. The lower δ 94/90 Zr NIST values (–0.45 to –0.04‰) of zircon from associated olivine-pyroxene hornblendites and orthopyroxenites are consistent with their origin as earlier cumulates genetically linked to the Kinsman granitoids. Our results demonstrate that while sediment assimilation in the lower crust could be responsible for the peraluminous signature, FC is the dominant driver of compositional diversity in these systems.

item.title: Tropical mafic rock weathering, riverine lithium isotopes, and the evolution of Cenozoic seawater chemistry
item.dc:creator: Chris T.L. Cheung
Kohen W. Bauer
Brian Beaty
Juan Miguel Guotana
Decibel V. Faustino-Eslava
Boriana Kalderon-Asael
Dan Asael
Karen Luk
Giancarlo DeFrancesco
Sean M. Newby
Yonghui Qin
Noah J. Planavsky
N. Ryan McKenzie
item.description: The terrestrial dissolved riverine lithium isotope (δ 7 Li diss ) flux can provide valuable information on silicate weathering processes, which are directly tied to the long-term carbon cycle, at various spatiotemporal scales. However, a limited understanding of how bedrock lithological composition impacts δ 7 Li diss values hinders the utility of the Li proxy for deep-time weathering reconstructions. Accordingly, we investigated tropical rivers in the Philippines and Mariana Islands that drain either distinctly ultramafic-to-mafic or silicic bedrock. We find that mafic lithologies produce markedly heavier δ 7 Li diss than those draining silicic lithologies, potentially due to preferential formation of Mg-rich clays during incongruent weathering. Furthermore, we observe that known tectonically driven changes in Earth’s global ophiolitic-collisional suture length correlate with the marine δ 7 Li record over the past ∼60 million years. This observation, supported by mass-balance modelling, indicates that variations in mafic bedrock weathering could have been an important influence on Cenozoic seawater chemistry. Published: 2026-03-19T14:12:32+00:00 Venue: Earth and Planetary Science Letters Score: 0.561 (consider)
item.content:encoded:
Authors: Chris T.L. Cheung; Kohen W. Bauer; Brian Beaty; Juan Miguel Guotana; Decibel V. Faustino-Eslava; Boriana Kalderon-Asael; Dan Asael; Karen Luk; Giancarlo DeFrancesco; Sean M. Newby; Yonghui Qin; Noah J. Planavsky; N. Ryan McKenzie

Journal: Earth and Planetary Science Letters

DOI: 10.1016/j.epsl.2026.119917

Relevance: 0.561 (consider)

The terrestrial dissolved riverine lithium isotope (δ 7 Li diss ) flux can provide valuable information on silicate weathering processes, which are directly tied to the long-term carbon cycle, at various spatiotemporal scales. However, a limited understanding of how bedrock lithological composition impacts δ 7 Li diss values hinders the utility of the Li proxy for deep-time weathering reconstructions. Accordingly, we investigated tropical rivers in the Philippines and Mariana Islands that drain either distinctly ultramafic-to-mafic or silicic bedrock. We find that mafic lithologies produce markedly heavier δ 7 Li diss than those draining silicic lithologies, potentially due to preferential formation of Mg-rich clays during incongruent weathering. Furthermore, we observe that known tectonically driven changes in Earth’s global ophiolitic-collisional suture length correlate with the marine δ 7 Li record over the past ∼60 million years. This observation, supported by mass-balance modelling, indicates that variations in mafic bedrock weathering could have been an important influence on Cenozoic seawater chemistry.

item.title: Combining Numerical Simulation and Seismology to Unravel the Exhumation Mechanism and Pathways of the Yardoi Dome, Eastern Himalayas
item.dc:creator: Xinglang Yin
Xiao Xu
Xiaoyu Guo
Chunsen Li
Xiaofei Tong
Bo Xiang
Zizhao Yuan
Yanqi Lin
Hongcheng Shi
item.description: Abstract The mechanisms of rapid exhumation of North Himalayan gneiss domes remain debated. We integrate phase equilibrium modeling, teleseismic tomography, and geodynamic simulation to reconstruct episodic exhumation paths and elucidate the underlying mechanisms. Our results confirm the previously inferred P‐T‐t evolution for the mid‐upper crust Greater Himalayan Sequence (GHS), featuring rapid near‐isothermal decompression during retrograde metamorphism followed by near‐isobaric cooling. Geodynamic modeling demonstrates that sustained intra‐crustal high temperatures trigger pervasive partial melting and pronounced rheological weakening within the middle‐lower level of upper crust. Consequently, extensive crustal melting and associated weakening are prerequisites for enabling exhumation. Sustained convergence and resistance from the Lhasa terrane induce prolonged shortening and thickening of the weakened crust. This culminates in rapid exhumation, driving upper‐crustal extension. Modeling reveals two primary exhumation pathways: (a) direct emplacement near the suture zone, forming the Yardoi dome, and (b) lateral migration facilitated by upper‐crustal shear zones along low‐viscosity channels, transporting material to the GHS and beyond. We conclude that crustal weakening induced by partial melting, in conjunction with the ongoing convergence of the Indian Plate, constitutes the primary control on multi‐stage exhumation events in the Himalayan orogen. The occurrence of multiple exhumation episodes requires successive thermal perturbations to regenerate these low‐viscosity zones. Published: 2026-03-16T05:56:02+00:00 Venue: Tectonics Score: 0.556 (consider)

Journal: Tectonics

DOI: 10.1029/2025tc009090

Relevance: 0.556 (consider)

Abstract The mechanisms of rapid exhumation of North Himalayan gneiss domes remain debated. We integrate phase equilibrium modeling, teleseismic tomography, and geodynamic simulation to reconstruct episodic exhumation paths and elucidate the underlying mechanisms. Our results confirm the previously inferred P‐T‐t evolution for the mid‐upper crust Greater Himalayan Sequence (GHS), featuring rapid near‐isothermal decompression during retrograde metamorphism followed by near‐isobaric cooling. Geodynamic modeling demonstrates that sustained intra‐crustal high temperatures trigger pervasive partial melting and pronounced rheological weakening within the middle‐lower level of upper crust. Consequently, extensive crustal melting and associated weakening are prerequisites for enabling exhumation. Sustained convergence and resistance from the Lhasa terrane induce prolonged shortening and thickening of the weakened crust. This culminates in rapid exhumation, driving upper‐crustal extension. Modeling reveals two primary exhumation pathways: (a) direct emplacement near the suture zone, forming the Yardoi dome, and (b) lateral migration facilitated by upper‐crustal shear zones along low‐viscosity channels, transporting material to the GHS and beyond. We conclude that crustal weakening induced by partial melting, in conjunction with the ongoing convergence of the Indian Plate, constitutes the primary control on multi‐stage exhumation events in the Himalayan orogen. The occurrence of multiple exhumation episodes requires successive thermal perturbations to regenerate these low‐viscosity zones.

item.title: Comparing Slab Viscous Dissipation Under Slab Distributed Weakening and Slab Segmentation Scenarios
item.dc:creator: Wenfa Zhou
Jiashun Hu
Keqing Li
Ting Yang
item.description: Abstract Subduction of cold, dense oceanic lithosphere is a key driver of plate tectonics and mantle convection. However, how much gravitational potential energy is internally dissipated by slab deformation during subduction remains debated and largely depends on the mechanisms of slab weakening. We develop two‐dimensional geodynamic models to systematically investigate viscous dissipation within subducting slabs under two end‐member scenarios: slab distributed weakening controlled by plastic yielding, and slab segmentation controlled by outer‐rise faulting and grain size damage. In distributed weakening models, ∼20% of the gravitational potential energy is consumed by slab viscous dissipation, which is independent of the viscosity of the slab, consistent with previous laboratory and numerical studies. In segmentation models caused by grain size evolution and serpentinization, slab viscous dissipation increases significantly, with the dissipation ratio reaching up to 40% . Slab segmentation alters the relationship between viscous dissipation and both effective viscosity and subduction velocity, breaking the conventional scaling laws based on fixed slab geometry. Moreover, by calculating viscous dissipation in global subduction zones, we infer that the average viscosity of subducting slabs does not exceed if slab segmentation is the dominant mode for global subduction zones. We estimate that 60%–80% of the gravitational potential energy is transferred to the surrounding mantle to drive mantle convection, regardless of the slab mode. These findings emphasize that accounting for more realistic rheological structures, including plastic yielding and slab segmentation in subduction models, is crucial for accurately quantifying energy partitioning within the Earth and understanding plate–mantle interactions. Published: 2026-03-17T10:34:47+00:00 Venue: Geochemistry, Geophysics, Geosystems Score: 0.554 (consider)
item.content:encoded:
Authors: Wenfa Zhou; Jiashun Hu; Keqing Li; Ting Yang

Journal: Geochemistry, Geophysics, Geosystems

DOI: 10.1029/2025gc012783

Relevance: 0.554 (consider)

Abstract Subduction of cold, dense oceanic lithosphere is a key driver of plate tectonics and mantle convection. However, how much gravitational potential energy is internally dissipated by slab deformation during subduction remains debated and largely depends on the mechanisms of slab weakening. We develop two‐dimensional geodynamic models to systematically investigate viscous dissipation within subducting slabs under two end‐member scenarios: slab distributed weakening controlled by plastic yielding, and slab segmentation controlled by outer‐rise faulting and grain size damage. In distributed weakening models, ∼20% of the gravitational potential energy is consumed by slab viscous dissipation, which is independent of the viscosity of the slab, consistent with previous laboratory and numerical studies. In segmentation models caused by grain size evolution and serpentinization, slab viscous dissipation increases significantly, with the dissipation ratio reaching up to 40% . Slab segmentation alters the relationship between viscous dissipation and both effective viscosity and subduction velocity, breaking the conventional scaling laws based on fixed slab geometry. Moreover, by calculating viscous dissipation in global subduction zones, we infer that the average viscosity of subducting slabs does not exceed if slab segmentation is the dominant mode for global subduction zones. We estimate that 60%–80% of the gravitational potential energy is transferred to the surrounding mantle to drive mantle convection, regardless of the slab mode. These findings emphasize that accounting for more realistic rheological structures, including plastic yielding and slab segmentation in subduction models, is crucial for accurately quantifying energy partitioning within the Earth and understanding plate–mantle interactions.

item.title: Neodymium stable isotope fractionation in minerals: Implications for Earth’s differentiation, and planetary formation
item.dc:creator: Mark Nestmeyer
Alex J. McCoy-West
item.description: The application of rare Earth element stable isotope compositions has become of increasing interest in geochemistry. Recently, studies have begun exploring variations in the stable 146 Nd /144 Nd isotope ratio in geological samples, with limited isotope fractionation observed in igneous rocks but significantly larger fractionations seen in low temperature systems. Experimental and theoretical studies on the equilibrium isotope fractionation of Nd are widely missing which can support understanding the fractionation of Nd isotopes among Earth’s major reservoirs. Here, we have modelled the isotope fractionation factors for 15 common rock forming and accessory minerals to help understand equilibrium stable isotope fractionation during medium to high temperature processes. Published: 2026-03-16T16:59:08+00:00 Venue: Geochimica et Cosmochimica Acta Score: 0.548 (consider)
item.content:encoded:
Authors: Mark Nestmeyer; Alex J. McCoy-West

Journal: Geochimica et Cosmochimica Acta

DOI: 10.1016/j.gca.2026.03.022

Relevance: 0.548 (consider)

The application of rare Earth element stable isotope compositions has become of increasing interest in geochemistry. Recently, studies have begun exploring variations in the stable 146 Nd /144 Nd isotope ratio in geological samples, with limited isotope fractionation observed in igneous rocks but significantly larger fractionations seen in low temperature systems. Experimental and theoretical studies on the equilibrium isotope fractionation of Nd are widely missing which can support understanding the fractionation of Nd isotopes among Earth’s major reservoirs. Here, we have modelled the isotope fractionation factors for 15 common rock forming and accessory minerals to help understand equilibrium stable isotope fractionation during medium to high temperature processes.

item.title: Geochemical characteristics of lithium in geothermal waters and its impact on riverine Li in the southeastern Tibetan Plateau
item.dc:creator: Longfei Xu
Yishan Cheng
Jinchuan Zhang
Yang Liu
Xingxu Zhao
Yuhang Sun
item.description: Variations in seawater lithium isotopes (δ 7 Li) are commonly linked to changes in continental silicate weathering fluxes. However, the influence of geothermal fluid input on riverine lithium, particularly in tectonically active regions, remains poorly constrained. Here, samples from hot springs along the Lancang River (LCR) in the southeastern Tibetan Plateau (TP) were collected for analysis of hydrochemistry, Li isotope composition (δ 7 Li) and water-rock interactions to study the geochemical characteristics of Li in geothermal waters and corresponding impact on riverine Li. Geothermal waters exhibit high Li concentrations (Li geo , 0.04–2.1 mg/L) and low Li isotope ratios (δ 7 Li geo , −0.56 to +12.3‰). High reservoir temperatures along the LCR (93–176 °C) facilitate water-rock interaction and leach more Li from surrounding rocks into geothermal waters. The Li geo and δ 7 Li geo are influenced by fluid residence time and the dissolution-precipitation balance. Geothermal waters with longer residence time have higher Li geo concentrations, whereas secondary mineral formation preferentially sequesters light Li, resulting in the enrichment of isotopically heavy Li in the residual geothermal fluid. The LCR exhibits elevated mean Li concentration (Li diss ) and depressed mean Li isotope ratio (δ 7 Li diss ) relative to global river averages, with both parameters tracking downstream variations in geothermal inputs. The mixing model estimates that the contribution of geothermal waters decreases downstream. The concomitant downstream increase in δ 7 Li diss may reflect either declining geothermal contribution or increased secondary mineral formation. Our study provides evidence for the significant impact of geothermal water on riverine Li, and therefore potentially linked to changes in seawater δ 7 Li records through Earth's history. Published: 2026-03-20T00:44:17+00:00 Venue: Chemical Geology Score: 0.545 (consider)
item.content:encoded:
Authors: Longfei Xu; Yishan Cheng; Jinchuan Zhang; Yang Liu; Xingxu Zhao; Yuhang Sun

Journal: Chemical Geology

DOI: 10.1016/j.chemgeo.2026.123378

Relevance: 0.545 (consider)

Variations in seawater lithium isotopes (δ 7 Li) are commonly linked to changes in continental silicate weathering fluxes. However, the influence of geothermal fluid input on riverine lithium, particularly in tectonically active regions, remains poorly constrained. Here, samples from hot springs along the Lancang River (LCR) in the southeastern Tibetan Plateau (TP) were collected for analysis of hydrochemistry, Li isotope composition (δ 7 Li) and water-rock interactions to study the geochemical characteristics of Li in geothermal waters and corresponding impact on riverine Li. Geothermal waters exhibit high Li concentrations (Li geo , 0.04–2.1 mg/L) and low Li isotope ratios (δ 7 Li geo , −0.56 to +12.3‰). High reservoir temperatures along the LCR (93–176 °C) facilitate water-rock interaction and leach more Li from surrounding rocks into geothermal waters. The Li geo and δ 7 Li geo are influenced by fluid residence time and the dissolution-precipitation balance. Geothermal waters with longer residence time have higher Li geo concentrations, whereas secondary mineral formation preferentially sequesters light Li, resulting in the enrichment of isotopically heavy Li in the residual geothermal fluid. The LCR exhibits elevated mean Li concentration (Li diss ) and depressed mean Li isotope ratio (δ 7 Li diss ) relative to global river averages, with both parameters tracking downstream variations in geothermal inputs. The mixing model estimates that the contribution of geothermal waters decreases downstream. The concomitant downstream increase in δ 7 Li diss may reflect either declining geothermal contribution or increased secondary mineral formation. Our study provides evidence for the significant impact of geothermal water on riverine Li, and therefore potentially linked to changes in seawater δ 7 Li records through Earth's history.

item.title: Burbankite-bearing ferrocarbonatite and its alteration to LREE ore at Swartbooisdrift, NW Namibia
item.dc:creator: Albert Riehm
Kirsten Drüppel
Lars Lenhart
Regina Mertz-Kraus
Rainer Ellmies
item.description: Based on compiled mineralogical and geochemical data, we show that burbankite-rich ferrocarbonatite is altered to calcite–bastnäsite–monazite ore at Swartbooisdrift, NW Namibia. The magmatic carbonatite assemblage comprises ankerite, magnetite, pyrochlore, fluorapatite, and locally abundant burbankite. All of these minerals can be enriched in layers that define a magmatic flow banding. Burbankite formation can be restrained to ~ 650 °C. Late-magmatic to hydrothermal alteration proceeds in situ from burbankite to carbocernaite (I to II) ± calcite ± barite ± strontianite, then cordylite-(Ce), monazite-(Ce) and finally ancylite-(Ce). Pyrochlore alters to columbite-(Fe) and fluorapatite to secondary monazite-(Ce). The calcite–bastnäsite–monazite ore forms below ~ 460 °C as veins, lenses and bands confined to the main ferrocarbonatite dikes. Its pinkish color results from minor hematite. Mass-balance calculations indicate fluid-assisted removal of Sr, Na, Mg, Ba and addition of Ca, P, Fe, Mn and LREE with modest volume loss to reach ore grades. C-O stable isotope analyses for the ferrocarbonatite, burbankite and LREE ore show an increasingly heavier δ18O signature linked to the hydrothermal alteration with an interpreted rising crustal influence and decreasing temperatures. Additionally, in-situ trace element analysis further provides arguments for the hydrothermal character of LREE redistribution through fluid-sensitive element ratios (Th/U; Y/Ho; Pb/Th). Our data suggest that formation of the Swartbooisdrift LREE ore results from instability and reaction of early Na-carbonates, apatite, and pyrochlore with progressive fluid evolution (decreasing Na and increasing oxygen fugacity with decreasing T), finally leading to a transformation of burbankite-rich zones of ferrocarbonatite into pink ore along carbonatite-hosted structures. This establishes a coherent magmatic–hydrothermal pathway from primary burbankite to economic LREE mineralization. Published: 2026-03-16T14:12:20+00:00 Venue: Mineralium Deposita Score: 0.542 (consider)
item.content:encoded:
Authors: Albert Riehm; Kirsten Drüppel; Lars Lenhart; Regina Mertz-Kraus; Rainer Ellmies

Journal: Mineralium Deposita

DOI: 10.1007/s00126-026-01441-8

Relevance: 0.542 (consider)

Based on compiled mineralogical and geochemical data, we show that burbankite-rich ferrocarbonatite is altered to calcite–bastnäsite–monazite ore at Swartbooisdrift, NW Namibia. The magmatic carbonatite assemblage comprises ankerite, magnetite, pyrochlore, fluorapatite, and locally abundant burbankite. All of these minerals can be enriched in layers that define a magmatic flow banding. Burbankite formation can be restrained to ~ 650 °C. Late-magmatic to hydrothermal alteration proceeds in situ from burbankite to carbocernaite (I to II) ± calcite ± barite ± strontianite, then cordylite-(Ce), monazite-(Ce) and finally ancylite-(Ce). Pyrochlore alters to columbite-(Fe) and fluorapatite to secondary monazite-(Ce). The calcite–bastnäsite–monazite ore forms below ~ 460 °C as veins, lenses and bands confined to the main ferrocarbonatite dikes. Its pinkish color results from minor hematite. Mass-balance calculations indicate fluid-assisted removal of Sr, Na, Mg, Ba and addition of Ca, P, Fe, Mn and LREE with modest volume loss to reach ore grades. C-O stable isotope analyses for the ferrocarbonatite, burbankite and LREE ore show an increasingly heavier δ18O signature linked to the hydrothermal alteration with an interpreted rising crustal influence and decreasing temperatures. Additionally, in-situ trace element analysis further provides arguments for the hydrothermal character of LREE redistribution through fluid-sensitive element ratios (Th/U; Y/Ho; Pb/Th). Our data suggest that formation of the Swartbooisdrift LREE ore results from instability and reaction of early Na-carbonates, apatite, and pyrochlore with progressive fluid evolution (decreasing Na and increasing oxygen fugacity with decreasing T), finally leading to a transformation of burbankite-rich zones of ferrocarbonatite into pink ore along carbonatite-hosted structures. This establishes a coherent magmatic–hydrothermal pathway from primary burbankite to economic LREE mineralization.
